lesson.menu_desc: Reading a quarterly report + present perfect -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 12 -- Quarterly Review -- Summarizing Results
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rafael Pelizaro | Aula 12 -- Quarterly Review

reading.rtitle: Q3 Project Review — Banco Atlas Migration
paras: Every quarter, our team stops to look back. We call it the quarterly review, and it has one simple goal: to recap what we have done and to plan what comes next. In the last three months, the Banco Atlas cloud migration has been our main objective, and I am happy to say the project is still on track.

Let me share the numbers. We set a target of moving forty systems to the cloud, and so far we have moved thirty-eight. Two key deliverables have gone live, and the average response time has improved by almost half. Our most important metric, customer satisfaction, has reached eighty-two percent. That result has exceeded the goal we agreed on in the summer.

Of course, the quarter has not been perfect. We have had one setback: a security test found a problem, so the final rollout has slipped by two weeks. Even so, the team has made real progress and the client has noticed. Next quarter our objective is clear — finish the migration, close the gap, and turn this achievement into a long-term contract.
reading.source: Adapted for class use from an internal IT project status report

gist.prompt: What is the best one-line summary of this report?
choices: The migration project has failed and the team has lost the client.
After a strong quarter, the project is on track and has beaten its main target, despite one small setback.
The team has finished the whole migration and already signed a new contract.

items: The team has already moved all forty systems to the cloud.
They have moved thirty-eight of the forty systems so far.
Customer satisfaction has exceeded the target the team agreed on.
Satisfaction reached eighty-two percent, which exceeded the summer goal.
A security setback has pushed the final rollout two weeks later.
A security test found a problem, so the rollout has slipped by two weeks.
The objective for next quarter is to start a brand-new project.
The objective is to finish the migration and win a long-term contract.
In your own work, how often does your team recap progress like this? Is it enough?
The report shares good news and one setback. Why is it important to give stakeholders both?
If you presented this quarter to the board, which result would you highlight first, and why?

listenings.text: Hi team, this is Rafael with a quick voice recap of the quarter before our review call, so everyone arrives on the same page. Overall, I am happy with where we are. The Banco Atlas migration has been our main objective, and the project is still on track. Let me give you the headline numbers. We set a target of forty systems in the cloud, and so far we have moved thirty-eight, so we are almost there. Two key deliverables have gone live this quarter, and the average response time has improved by nearly half. Our most important metric is customer satisfaction, and it has reached eighty-two percent. That figure has exceeded the goal we agreed on back in the summer, so that is a real achievement for the whole team. I will be honest about the setback too. A security test found a problem late in the quarter, so the final rollout has slipped by about two weeks. It is not ideal, but the team has made strong progress and the client has noticed the difference. For the review meeting, please come ready to talk about three things: how we close the gap on the last two systems, how we recover the two weeks, and how we turn this quarter into a long-term contract. Thanks, everyone.

Hi Rafael, this is Diana from the steering committee, leaving you a short voicemail after I read your quarterly summary. First of all, well done. The numbers are clear and honest, and the board likes that. Eighty-two percent on customer satisfaction is excellent, and I was glad to see you flag the security setback openly instead of hiding it. A couple of points for the review meeting. On the two systems that are still pending, I would like a firm date, not just on track. The board has heard on track before, and they want a day on the calendar. On the rollout that has slipped, please bring one clear plan to recover the two weeks, and be ready to say what you need from us to make it happen. And on the contract, this is the moment. You have exceeded the target and the client is happy, so I want you to present the long-term proposal at the end of the review, while the good results are fresh in everyone's mind. Send me the final deck the day before, and we will talk on the call. Thanks, Rafael, and again, strong quarter.
